FT_IC1.evidence: Study uses Year 2 data from ABCD Study (2018-2020, ages 11-12, N=10,157), a U.S. national cohort

FT_IC2.evidence: Online dating (qualifying SM variable: platform-specific use). Single item: 'Have you ever used a dating app?' (yes/no). B1 applies.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multivariable logistic regression: AOR 2.72 (male), AOR 12.97 (sexual minority). Test of SM ↔ sociodemographic factors.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric extractable: AOR 2.72 (95% CI 1.11–6.78) for male sex; AOR 12.97 (95% CI 4.32–38.96) for sexual minority. Tables 1 and 2 with p-values.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis with SM coefficient reported (online dating vs. sociodemographic predictors).

FT_IC6.evidence: Original published research article, not a duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: All three FT-EC7 conditions hold: (a) Online dating is the DEPENDENT variable in every analysis (demographics → SM); no SM → non-SM-outcome analysis present. (b) All predictors are demographic-only (sex, race/ethnicity, age, household income, parental education, sexual orientation). (c) Cross-sectional data only (Year 2, 2018-2020). B15 carve-out does not apply (no longitudinal/prospective analysis present). FT-EC7 = TRUE.
notes: Research note design, small sample of SM users (0.4%, n=38), but fully meets all FT criteria. SM is dependent variable with demographic-only predictors, cross-sectional. Excludes for FT-EC7.

FT_IC1.evidence: Perspective/commentary article referencing Carter et al (2024) UK study of 657 16-18-year-olds from England (5 schools). Not U.S. ABCD Study data.

FT_IC2.evidence: Article discusses smartphone use and reduction strategies but does not conduct original quantitative SM/phone analysis.

FT_IC3.evidence: Opinion piece; no inferential testing of associations presented.

FT_IC4.evidence: No original numeric data extractable from this paper.

FT_IC5.evidence: Commentary/perspective article citing other studies. Not original empirical analysis. B14 applies (perspective, not research).

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT-IC1 (not U.S. ABCD), FT-IC5 (not original analysis). FT-EC7 not assessed.
notes: This is a perspective article on problematic smartphone use; it discusses ABCD findings mentioned by reference but does not conduct original ABCD analysis. Fails FT-IC1 and FT-IC5 (B14: perspective article).

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study data from 2019-2020 assessment (N=10,147, ages ~12 years), U.S. national cohort.

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying SM variables: type of apps used (TikTok, Instagram, Snapchat, YouTube, Facebook, Twitter, Reddit, Pinterest), time on SM, cyberbullying, secret accounts, problematic/addictive use (SMAQ-like scale). B1 applies (separate SM coefficients).

FT_IC3.evidence: Exposome-wide association study (ExWAS) with linear mixed-effects models testing SM exposures ↔ BPM Total Problem T-scores and suicide attempts. Multiple regression coefficients reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric extractable: β coefficients with 95% CI (e.g., cyberbullying β est., secret accounts, etc.; Table S1 lists 35 FDR-significant exposures). Model coefficients in Table 2 (digital exposomic risk: estimate = 1.78, 95% CI = [1.58–1.98], t = 17.41, p < 0.001). Suicide attempt OR = 1.76 (95% CI [1.39–2.23], z = 4.69, p < 0.001).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis with SM ↔ mental health outcomes (psychopathology and suicide attempts). Multiple SM coefficients reported separately.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Paper reports SM as INDEPENDENT variable (predictor of mental health outcomes), NOT as dependent variable. SM → non-SM-outcome analysis is primary. Fails condition (a) of FT-EC7.
notes: Robust original ABCD analysis using ExWAS approach. SM is IV predicting mental health outcomes (BPM, suicide attempts), not DV. B18 applies: SM-as-IV → outcome (cross-sectional). INCLUDE based on core FT criteria.
